metadata.summary: Event versioning, upcasters, event catalog as committed source, breaking-change gate, GDPR crypto-shredding, and PII handling in event stores.

content.title: Event Sourcing — Event Versioning and GDPR
content.summary: Once an event class is published (consumed by any downstream system), its field contract is immutable. Breaking changes require an upcaster and a version bump committed to the event catalog. PII in events must be crypto-shredded or moved to a mutable side table to satisfy GDPR right-to-erasure.

why.p: Event versioning is the most commonly underestimated operational challenge in event sourcing. Once consumers exist — projections, sagas, downstream microservices, audit exports — renaming or removing an event field silently breaks them. Most teams discover this on event #2 when they realize they cannot change the name of a field without coordinating a multi-team deployment or writing a migration that transforms stored event data.

GDPR right-to-erasure conflicts directly with the immutable append-only log. An erasure request on a customer who appears in 50,000 order events cannot be satisfied by deleting rows. The two viable strategies are crypto-shredding (encrypt PII fields per-subject, drop the key) and PII externalization (store only a customer_id in events, keep PII in a mutable side table that can be cleared).

list.item: Any event class that has been consumed by more than one system (projection, saga, external subscriber) — version it from day one.
Systems processing personal data (names, addresses, payment tokens, health records) — apply PII strategy before publishing the first event.
Teams adopting a schema registry (Apicurio, Confluent) — event catalog is the local complement.
When adding a new field to a published event class (always backward-compatible with default value) or removing/renaming one (requires upcaster).
Internal events consumed only by the same aggregate's own projection and never published externally — version overhead may not be warranted, but keep PII strategy regardless.
Pre-PMF systems with zero external consumers — establish the pattern early but do not over-engineer the catalog tooling.

rule.statement: Every published event class MUST have an event_version field (integer, default 1). Any field rename, removal, or semantic change MUST bump event_version and register an upcaster that transforms the old shape to the new shape.
rule.rationale: Consumers reading historical events will encounter old-version events even after the producer deploys the new shape. Without an upcaster, old events either fail deserialization or produce wrong data. The upcaster is the migration contract.

rule.statement: A committed .event-catalog.json (or equivalent schema registry export) MUST live in the repository. CI MUST fail when a published event class changes without a corresponding version bump and upcaster.
rule.rationale: Without a machine-enforceable catalog, breaking changes reach consumers silently. The catalog is the contract; the CI gate is the enforcement. Teams that rely on human review alone consistently miss schema breaks in PR review.

rule.statement: PII MUST NOT appear in event payloads without a crypto-shredding plan or PII externalization. Either encrypt PII fields per-subject (crypto-shredding: drop the key on erasure request) or store only an opaque ID in the event and keep PII in a mutable GDPR-compliant side table.
rule.rationale: GDPR Article 17 right-to-erasure requires deletion of personal data on request. An immutable event log cannot delete rows. Crypto-shredding and PII externalization are the two architecturally sound solutions; ignoring this results in regulatory exposure and impossible retrofits.

section.name: event-catalog-lint
section.p: The event catalog lint script blocks breaking changes to published events. Wire it into pre-commit so renames or field drops fail locally before reaching CI.

example.title: event-catalog-lint.py — block breaking changes to published events
example.code: #!/usr/bin/env python3 """event-catalog-lint.py — block breaking changes to published events. Usage: event-catalog-lint.py <event-catalog.json> <events-package-path> event-catalog.json shape: {"OrderPlaced": {"version": 1, "fields": ["order_id","customer_id","total"]}} """ import ast, json, sys, pathlib catalog = json.loads(pathlib.Path(sys.argv[1]).read_text()) pkg = pathlib.Path(sys.argv[2]) errs = [] for src in pkg.rglob("*.py"): tree = ast.parse(src.read_text()) for node in ast.walk(tree): if not isinstance(node, ast.ClassDef): continue if node.name not in catalog: continue recorded = catalog[node.name] # collect declared fields (dataclass attrs) fields = [a.target.id for a in node.body if isinstance(a, ast.AnnAssign) and isinstance(a.target, ast.Name)] missing = set(recorded["fields"]) - set(fields) renamed = set(fields) - set(recorded["fields"]) - {"event_version"} if missing: errs.append(f"{src}:{node.lineno} {node.name} dropped fields {sorted(missing)} " f"-- add upcaster, bump event_version") if renamed: errs.append(f"{src}:{node.lineno} {node.name} new fields {sorted(renamed)} " f"-- bump event_version, add upcaster from v{recorded['version']}") if errs: print("\n".join(errs)); sys.exit(1) print(f"event-catalog OK ({len(catalog)} events)")
example.note: Wire into pre-commit. The catalog file is committed; renames/drops fail CI until the developer acknowledges by bumping version and adding an upcaster.

example.title: .event-catalog.json example
example.note: Committed to the repo. CI blocks any PR that changes a listed event class without bumping its version and registering an upcaster.

section.name: upcasters
section.p: An upcaster is a pure function that transforms an event from version N to version N+1. The event store calls the appropriate upcaster chain when loading historical events so consumers always receive the current shape.

example.title: Upcaster pattern (Python)
example.code:
from typing import dict
def upcast_order_placed_v1_to_v2(event_data: dict) -> dict: """ v1: {order_id, customer_id, shipping_address, total_amount} v2: adds placed_at field (was missing in v1; backfill from occurred_at) """ result = dict(event_data) result["placed_at"] = event_data.get("occurred_at") result["event_version"] = 2 return result
# Registry maps (event_type, version) -> upcaster UPCASTERS = { ("OrderPlaced", 1): upcast_order_placed_v1_to_v2, }
def load_and_upcast(event_type: str, event_version: int, event_data: dict) -> dict: """Apply upcaster chain until we reach the current version.""" v = event_version data = dict(event_data) while (event_type, v) in UPCASTERS: data = UPCASTERS[(event_type, v)](data) v = data["event_version"] return data
example.note: Upcasters are pure functions — no DB access, no side effects. Chain them: v1→v2→v3 if multiple schema bumps exist.

section.name: gdpr-strategies
section.p: Crypto-shredding: encrypt PII fields in the event payload with a per-subject key stored in a separate erasable key store. On erasure request, delete the subject's encryption key. Historical events remain in the log but their PII fields become unreadable ciphertext.

PII externalization: store only an opaque identifier (customer_id, subject_id) in events. Keep all PII (name, address, email, payment tokens) in a mutable relational table that is subject to standard GDPR delete operations. Events reference the subject by ID; the PII table is cleared on erasure request.
Both approaches require a decision before publishing the first event containing personal data. Retrofitting either strategy to an existing event log is a major migration. Choose at design time.

antipattern.description: Copying command payload directly into event fields without a PII audit step — e.g. OrderPlaced(shipping_address={"name": "Alice", "email": "alice@example.com", ...}).
antipattern.reason: Shipping addresses and emails are personal data. Once in the immutable event log without crypto-shredding, they cannot be erased. A future GDPR erasure request becomes technically impossible to satisfy without corrupting the event store.

antipattern.description: Adding a field to a published event class "just to make a query easier" without bumping event_version and registering an upcaster.
antipattern.reason: Consumers reading historical events receive old-shape events. If they expect the new field, they get AttributeError or None silently. The version bump + upcaster is the contract that allows consumers to upgrade independently.

antipattern.description: Deleting or renaming a published event class without a coordinated multi-consumer deprecation and replacement plan.
antipattern.reason: Consumers depend on the event class name for routing and deserialization. Silent removal breaks all consumers simultaneously. Human review is required — never auto-merge event class deletions.

section.name: references-versioning
section.p: Key references for event versioning and GDPR in event-sourced systems:

list.item: Vernon, V. "Implementing Domain-Driven Design," ch. 8 (Domain Events).
Apicurio / Confluent Schema Registry — schema versioning and compatibility checks.
